content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 16, 1990


Hunt Braly
Administrative Assistant
State Senator Ed Davis
19th Senate District Office
11145 Tampa Avenue, Suite 21B
Northridge, CA 91326

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-596

Dear Mr. Braly:

This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
State Senator Ed Davis and his campaign committee concerning the
permissible use of his campaign funds under the Political Reform
Act ("the Act").1/ This letter confirms the telephone advice
provided to you on Thursday, September 20, 1990.

QUESTION

May Senator Davis use his campaign funds to pay for the costs
of a conference sponsored by the senator, on the lobbying process
for local public officials in his district? These costs would
include overhead expenses, such as hotel conference room rental,
invitations, and postage; meals, transportation, and hotel
accommodations for the panelists (if required); and meals,
invitations and continental breakfast and lunch, for the invited
guests.

CONCLUSION

Expenditures made to sponsor a conference for local public
officials in the senator's district intended to provide
information about the lobbying process and to improve the
officials' ability to lobby more successfully in the state capitol

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

File No. A-90-596
Page 2


are directly related to a governmental purpose. Consequently,
Senator Davis may use his campaign funds to pay for the overhead
expenses of the conference, for the meals, transportation, and
hotel accommodations of the panelists, and for the meals of the
invited guests.

FACTS

Senator Davis desires to sponsor a conference on the lobbying
process for local public officials in his district this fall. He
frequently carries bills for local public agencies and believes
that these local officials and their staffs will benefit greatly
from a conference designed to educate and inform them on how to
lobby successfully in Sacramento.

The program will begin with a continental breakfast, be
followed by two panel discussions, and conclude with a luncheon at
which Senator Davis will be the featured speaker. The first panel
will be comprised of lobbyists from Sacramento, and the second
panel will consist of local government lobbyists.

Among those invited to attend the conference will be the
Ventura and Santa Barbara County Board of Supervisors, city
councils from every city in the senator's district, water and park
district board members, school district board members, including
community college districts, and, if space allows, local citizen
activists.

The costs will consist of overhead expenses, such as,
invitations, postage, and hotel conference room rental; panelists
expenses, such as, meals, transportation, and hotel room
accommodations (if required); and two meals, a continental
breakfast and lunch, for the invited guests.

ANALYSIS

Effective January 1, 1990, the Act was amended by Senate Bill
1431 to include new provisions that regulate the appropriate use
of campaign funds. (Section 85800, et seq.) The purpose of the
legislation was "to ensure that candidates, elected officers and
the people clearly recognize acceptable and unacceptable uses of
campaign funds." (Senate Elections Committee Analysis of SB
1431.)

The general rule of the new personal use law is that any
expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a minimum, reasonably
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(Section 85801.) However, where an expenditure confers a
substantial benefit on the candidate or officeholder, the
expenditure must be directly related to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose. (Section 85801.) A substantial personal
benefit is defined as a direct personal benefit to the candidate
or officeholder of $100 or more. (Section 85800(b)(2).)

File No. A-90-596
Page 3


     Moreover, the new personal use law sets forth provisions
dealing with specific types of expenditures. For example, Section
85802(b) provides that an expenditure of campaign funds for
professional services is permitted only when the services are
directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose. Section 85802(f)(1) provides, in pertinent part, that an
expenditure of campaign funds for personal gifts is permitted only
when the gift is directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose.

     Where campaign funds are used to pay certain of the expenses
of the lobbying conference, such as the cost of the meals, hotel
room accommodation and transportation of the panelists, you will
be making a payment for professional services. Consequently, for
an expenditure of Senator Davis' campaign funds to be permissible
there must be a direct relationship between the expenditure for
the panelists' meals, transportation and accommodation and a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section
85202(b).)

     Where campaign funds are used to pay certain of the expenses
of the lobbying conference, such as the cost of the meals, the
continental breakfast and lunch, and any other benefits for the
invited guests, you will be making a gift to the public officials
and their staffs who attend the conference. The Act defines the
term "gift" to mean a payment to the extent that the acquisition of
equal or greater value is not received. (Section 82028.)
Consequently, for an expenditure of Senator Davis' campaign funds
to be permissible, there must be a direct relationship between the
expenditure for these meals and a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. (Section 85802(f)(1).)

     Where campaign funds are used to pay overhead expenses of the
conference, such as the cost of the invitations, postage, hotel
conference room rental, and any educational materials, you will
not be making a gift under the Act. Section 82028(b)(1) excludes
informational materials from the definition of a gift. (Douglas
Advice Letter, No. A-90-128, copy enclosed.) For an expenditure
of Senator Davis' campaign funds to be permissible for these
type of overhead costs, there must be a reasonable relationship to
a legislative or governmental purpose.

     Your inquiry, therefore, requires a determination whether
each of the costs involved herein is either directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose, or, as in the
case of overhead expenses, reasonably related to a legislative or
governmental purpose. Since the purpose of the conference is to
educate and inform local public officials and their staffs on how
to lobby more successfully in their state capital, it is intended
to improve the officials' ability to perform their official
duties. Therefore, we conclude that the conference itself is
directly related to a governmental purpose. Further, we conclude
that each of the costs designated by you in your letter also is

File No. A-90-596
Page 4


directly related to this conference, and, hence, to a governmental
purpose.

     Because it only recently assumed jurisdiction over the laws
regarding the permissible uses of campaign funds, the Commission
is considering regulations interpreting the "personal use" laws
for adoption by the Commission in the near future. At that time,
it is possible that the Commission may choose to adopt regulations
which may be at variance with the advice given in this letter.

     I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                   Sincerely,


                                   Scott Hallabrin
                                   Acting General Counsel

                                   [signature]
                                   By: Deanne Stone
                                       Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure
